svg.title: Six keys to a repeatable pickleball dink
svg.desc: Six numbered rows describing how to hit a dink. One, start in the position you want to finish in: feet shoulder width, weight on the balls of the feet, knees bent rather than bent at the hips, paddle up in front. Two, feet first and then paddle: shuffle sideways with your hips square to the net instead of crossing your feet over. Three, hold the paddle at about a three out of ten, because grip tension travels into the ball. Four, contact the ball in front of your body every time; contact beside or behind you sends the ball up. Five, push from the shoulder and lift with the legs, with a quiet wrist and almost no backswing. Six, recover to the kitchen line and back toward the middle after every dink.
svg.text: SIX KEYS TO A DINK YOU CAN REPEAT
EVERY ONE OF THEM IS ABOUT TAKING ENERGY OUT OF THE SHOT

g.text: START WHERE YOU WANT TO FINISH
Feet shoulder width, weight forward, knees bent. Bend at the knees, not the hips, or your eyes drop.
Shuffle sideways with your hips square to the net. Crossing your feet over opens a hole behind you.
GRIP AT A THREE OR FOUR OUT OF TEN
Squeeze and the tension runs down the handle into the face, and the ball leaves higher than you meant.
Beside you pops it up. Behind you sends your whole body up with it. Step back to keep the ball in front.
PUSH FROM THE SHOULDER, LIFT WITH THE LEGS
Quiet wrist, backswing of a few inches. Small joints and big swings are where the pop-ups come from.
RECOVER TO THE LINE, AND TO THE MIDDLE
The kitchen line is the best real estate on the court. Take a ball off it, then walk right back onto it.
